feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

paragraphs.text: As a child of the internet (born 1987), a computer scientist (phd) and father of 2 kids (7 and 12), and a daily user of software office apps, programming related systems, games, and digital creative tools (2D/3D). I have watched nervously as it seemed every step forward in technology seemed to be matched by an equally egregious and deliberate consumer-unfriendly strategy to earn more per user, all while legislation has not been able to keep up. I am happy to see work being done on this front. It is prudent and necessary to enforce some minimum standard in this regard on these companies.
My feedback will cover software subscription models, dark patterns, masked gambling, in-app currency distancing, and advertisement-based mobile apps aimed at children.

Over the past 20 years, we have seen companies move progressively more towards subscription based models. The obvious and stated problems are the difficulties with unsubscribing, Adobe being one of the companies often highlighted as one of the most egregious examples. Examples from the US has shown how companies deliberately make the effort to make it more difficult to cancel in states where this is not specifically prevented by law. Users would have a maze of buttons to click, unless selecting the state of California, then all of a sudden a single button unsubscribe was present due to a law in that state requiring ease of cancellation. I think this informs us in the EU that we should have broad and strong enforcement of a one-click cancel option.

On subscriptions, I think it is important to also consider that many purchasable products are being turned into subscription models. This is, in general, a consumer-unfriendly practice that, in most cases, seeks to hide unacceptable purchase prices as a long stream of continuous payments. Sometimes it is fair, in the sense that the product is updated live from the subscription amount, but often, for single purchase items, it is a way to manipulate the consumer to pay more than they would be willing to pay had they sat down with a personal finance expert and calculated the lifetime pay. How did we get to a point where car-seat heating hardware is present in the car at sale, but has to be enabled in software by a monthly payment (BMW)?

Further, the subscription models are being used as planned obsolescence destroying software once the company is ready with their next version (See the stop destroying videogames EU initiative https://eci.ec.europa.eu/045/public/#/screen/home). Like the initiative suggests, I think all software should be required to have an end-of-life plan for when the company doesn't think it is profitable to keep supporting the software.

On the topic of dark patterns, I see this mostly in games. One example that comes to mind was the Diablo 4 battle pass activation (Example: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9-BcnTbiuPU), which was a 1-click non-refundable purchase. Only due to the broad community backlash was this eventually changed. Many more examples exist. Due to the nature of these methods, how they can often be brushed off as accidental design, I fear it can be hard to legislate effectively.

On the topic of Masked gambling, as I call it, I am referring to what is commonly called lootboxes in games. You purchase a chance for a number of items. Since these lootboxes are often bought directly with money, or by an in-game currency purchasable by real-world money, and the inherent random nature of the outcome, it is surprising to me that this has

not been tried under gambling laws yet. Maybe direct legislation is needed? In gaming circles, high spenders have garnered the nickname of "whales", spending upwards of thousands of dollars, with cases in the game Diablo Immortal of spending above 100000 (one-hundred-thousand) dollars. Each to their own, but there are children in many of these games whose parents' credit cards are enforced-connected to the games, requiring systems of permissive allow/disallow parental control systems. We learn as parents to set boundaries, but preferably, some of the worst of these practices would be made illegal. I would personally outlaw any chance-based reward systems if they have a possible conversion to real-world currency. E.g., if a system is random, no monetary purchase should directly or indirectly give more chances or increased chances in this system. This can be subtly hidden in the game, so I think the law has to be written broadly like this.

On in-app currency distancing, what I refer to is the specific use of digital currency as a manipulative strategy to get users to not realize how much they pay. In many software systems, such as a cloud-based system like Azure or similar, and in-app/in-game stores. Digital currencies are used as a substitute for real money with the explicit purpose of making it difficult to calculate the real money equivalent of a purchase. A number of unsavory practices are employed. One is how price increases can be hidden since there are two places to increase the price, either in the purchase of the digital currency or in the conversion from digital currency to a digital product. Did it cost 8.95 EUR to buy 100 digital credits, or did it cost 9.95? Did the in-app purchase cost 109 or 119 digital credits? Similarly, as I have shown on this example, digital credit bundles are often set to an amount which does not nicely convert to in-app digital credits, resulting having to purchase multiple lower value packages for single purchase, or over-purchase a larger package for large-bundle amount "saving", both cases resulting in left-over credits in the in-app account, giving the user a feeling of wasting credits, which has been shown through studies to drastically increase the chance of the next purchase. Most commonly, it is not possible to convert the digital credits back to real-world money. I would personally dispense with digital currency bought from real-world currency entirely, enforcing the use of real-world currency listing in in-app stores in native currency. Be that USD, EUR, DKK, etc. This would make the value calculation direct and resolve the issue with leftover unspent/locked credits.

On advertising in mobile games. Many children today, as young as 3+ years, spend far too many hours on a mobile phone. This Act does not address this directly. However, many of these mobile app games have a nearly 50/50 split between advertisement and playing the game. It is horrible. I don't know if this could somehow be covered under this act. On one side, the advertisements are designed to hide the cancel button, which is a clear example of a dark pattern. Case in point, a child plays a game, something new happens in the game, triggering an automatic ad to play. Commonly, no close button is shown for a few seconds, sometimes one is visible with a timer, sometimes it is the same color as the background, making it close to invisible. Often it is placed so close to the edge of the screen that it is hard to tap it, and it is always so small that the likelihood of clicking next to it makes it so that you click the ad, bringing you to the app store for that advertised app, is high. These ads often have multiple screens to click or wait through. For mobile app games, the ad itself is commonly a version of the game, with 3 distinct screens. First, you watch an unskippable video about the game, then you are forced to play a small version of it, then you are moved to a splash screen which advertises a download button. Having to do this once every minute cannot be healthy for anyone, especially not children.

As a software engineer working in a company where it has been decided to make our app a SaaS (Software as a Service) app, I can tell you these decisions server only to increase profits. There is no tangible benefit for the user in vast majority of cases. Industry organisations will call it prohibitively expensive to change these practices, but this is not the case. It is true that these ill-gotten gains will register as a loss to the companies, but there are a vast number of smaller or independent companies that manage to successfully write similar software without using these practices. Enforcing strict practices here will benefit both developers and consumers. I, for one, would welcome it with open arms.
Thank you for the opportunity to give feedback on this important topic.
